title: RA.974 Black Rave Culture
tracklist: Hour 1: Nativesun L-Vis 1990 - OUTSIDE TRAK Black Rave Culture - Herengracht 164 [Unreleased] Ossie - Now's The Time 1OO1O - Tamarindo Dlala Thukzin - Whistle DJ SWISHA - BIRDMAN LUGZ Kade Young - GGGO 619! - Highway Track Effy - Mad T.NO - SON TIN Truncate - Jerk Jordan Gardner - Rhythm Acceleration (Omaar Remix) Tony Quattro - Bruce Gonna Blow Toura - 44 Bongo Kush Jones - Pink Grapefruit AceMo - Unreleased Kanyon - RoadRunner Mmolotsi Mabua - The World Is A Ghetto Elbarto - Yehnaioui Nikki Nair, Nativesun, Escaflowne, James Bangura, Ray Kandinski - Work That [Unreleased] Jossy Mitsu - Arena Black Rave Culture - Jambu Leonce x Girl Unit - Thankful Tah - DRM-973 Liano - Elektron Physical Therapy & Nick Leon - Genesis (DJ SWISHA Remix) Taken - Drumcode Hour 2: James Bangura Mark Ambrose - Groove X Dauwd - The Yuzer (Dauwd Deep Dub Mix) James Bangura x ZDBT - Head Down Low [Unreleased] Sub Basics - Effect Dario Zenker - The Edit FJAAK - Unity (G's Dub) Danny Wabbit - When Giraffes Fly AWood - Orbit MoMA Ready - BLACK PULSE Kyle Hall - Told Them Before G Flame - Mango Girls James Bangura - Cavernous Sad [Unreleased] Shed - UFO2 Ian Pooley - Viola Jackin Trax & Don Rimini - Booty Ride William Kiss - Werkin' PUSHMANN & Fixon - Tribalism 04 Truncate - The Way I Am Hour 3: Amal GATEX RATEX DJ - PASSAT Avi Loud - GIBBERISH Black Rave Culture - Hong Thai Dub [Unreleased] Bianca Oblivion, XL Mad - Bubble Pon Di Bed (CHALE DUB) Pl4net Dust - Novinha Boo BURNA - Crushed GYRO! - Dreamland Dance (Calm Stiege Remix) ??? - FIRST TIME AROUND THE WORLD RAFAEL - PRESSURE Davina - Don't You Want It (Extended Mix) Tromac x Kooxla - HATERS Black Rave Culture - Dick Control TSHA - BOYZ DJ Suplex - And The Smoove Baby Storm KMS - Chandelier Gene Richards Jr, Fixon - Apache BASSBEAR!! x DJ SWISHA x Kanyon - Eric Phresh Go Hiyama - Peg Samuel L. - Danshes D'Afrique Black Jazz Chronicles - Space Prom

translation.content: "If the baby boomer generation had the three from Belleville, millennials can say… we have Black Rave Culture (BRC)." Lofty praise from Spain's WOS, and yet the undeniable buzz surrounding BRC makes it feel merited. The Washington, D.C. trio of Amal, James Bangura and Nativesun are straight shooters and stone-cold chillers, navigating their well-deserved success with joie de vivre. In their hands, a good time is always guaranteed. The BRC experience is physical from start to finish, as the trio glide through the rich canon of Black dance music. As you'll hear on RA.974, BRC push big, badass blends and seat-of-your-pants mixing. You'll hear them slamming ghettotech into gqom, threading UK garage through East Coast club, stitching silky amapiano and swung techno, and landing the odd all-timer with fervour and flair. BRC's productions mine a similar store of energy, and you'll find plenty of those here too. Perhaps most importantly, their tangible chemistry and a sincere, undimmed enthusiasm for tunes are what makes this group so damn magnetic. Without looking, it's difficult to know who's DJing during sets, a testament to their cohesion. And while their RA Podcast is split threeways, it could just as easily be a round-robin session behind the decks. There's a spiritual link with other genre-splicing, all-action crews active along the East Coast right now, most obviously New York's Kush Jones, DJ SWISHA, AceMo and MoMa Ready. In BRC's case, their DIY attitude and community mentality underline their down-to-earth appeal. Maximum vibes is what the BFFs in BRC deliver in spades, with this mix an exhilarating three-hour journey through creating serious dance floor pressure and having a ball in the process. - Nyshka Chandran What have you been up to recently? Honestly, we're just staying busy, from moving studio spaces to curating a remix record of our previous work. 2025 started pretty turbulent with the change in the administration but now's the time to stay focused despite what's going on. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as artists? We'd have to say Moonshine out of Montreal. They created something that was missing and built a true institution. We asked for a three-hour mix to reflect your trio status, and you've broken it down exactly at one hour a piece. Walk us through what each hour entails. Nativesun: I wanted my hour to set the tone, like that moment you walk into the club. From the first track that has you all the way locked in, to where you just wanna stay right there and see what's coming next. Seamless groove of tracks that standout. James Bangura: I've been listening to a lot of mixes from DJs I really enjoy. So for my section, I was trying to focus on maintaining tension throughout while also keeping it funky and light. Amal: My approach was to keep the energy up and end off with a bang. Still within the same realm sonically, with a couple of curveballs to wake folks up at the end, like how a late-night closing set feels. Your productions on the Black Rave Culture series cast a wide net, reflecting what you play out in DJ sets. Are there any new sounds you're thinking of exploring in the studio? We've been incorporating a lot more Afro and Latin diasporic sounds like 3-step, gqom, batida and kuduro. I think more than anything, we like to take the old stuff and put it into a new context. Is there a Vol 4 on the way? As of now, no. We do have a remix EP for Vol 3 alongside a bunch of brand-new singles slated for the rest of the year! You got started in a city with relatively few venues for underground club music. What advice would you give to up-and-coming artists who are facing the same issue? The best advice we can give is to do it yourself. It may sound daunting but you have more power than you think. The community will come because they will feel it. There are no rules and no right approach to expressing yourself through music—don't be afraid to do something new and rewrite the norm! Are there any Black rave or Black electronic artists—past, present or future—you especially want to sound out? Who should people be paying extra attention to? When we played in Brazil we met a super talented artist, Africanoise, who has a track on our forthcoming remix album. Vanyfox has been honing the batida sound and inspiring us, and flotussin is a sick DJ out of Baltimore—she's been ripping up dance floors. Also, De Schuurman from the Netherlands is pushing the Dutch Bubbling sound. Just finally: DJ Ess, a young Texas-bred producer is a breath of fresh air mixing underground rap and electronic music, a pioneer of the new "jerk" wave. Finally, what are you looking forward to this year? We're excited to play shows with our homies, write more music, and show the world what we're capable of.
translation.blurb: Three hours of high-intensity heaters from the trio lighting up US club music.
